text.title: Methodologies Summary (Product Manager) Core Rules
text.summary: Routing rubric matching observable PM-task signals (artifact, time horizon, decision shape) to the right methodology under pro/product/product-manager/.

rule.statement: Routing MUST be deterministic given the three signals (artifact, horizon, decision_shape). Two agents with the same inputs MUST pick the same methodology.
rule.rationale: Non-deterministic routing erodes trust and produces inconsistent outputs across runs.

rule.statement: The first routing dimension is the requested artifact (spec / roadmap / OKR / experiment / readout / pricing decision). Artifact shape eliminates most candidates immediately.
rule.rationale: Most PM tasks are artifact-driven; routing on artifact first prunes the search space cheaply.

rule.statement: Time horizon (this-week / this-sprint / this-quarter / annual) is the second dimension. Mismatched horizon is the most common mis-routing.
rule.rationale: Quarterly methodologies applied weekly produce noise; weekly methodologies stretched to annual collapse.

rule.statement: Decision shape (binary / ranked / cascade / framework-pick / report) breaks any remaining tie.
rule.rationale: Decision shape captures the operator's mental model of 'what answer do I want'; the rubric must mirror that.

rule.statement: When no methodology fits cleanly, the rubric MUST name `methodologies-summary` as the fallback (this methodology) rather than guessing a near-match.
rule.rationale: Silent guessing routes ambiguous tasks to plausibly-relevant methodologies that produce wrong outputs; named fallback is honest.

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — task already tagged with a methodology slug; agent inside another methodology's procedure; non-PM-domain task), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
